eli3: Seasonality is a systematic, periodic pattern in a time series that repeats at regular intervals (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, annually). It is distinct from trend (long-run direction) and irregular fluctuations (random noise). Example: retail sales spike every December, flu incidence peaks in winter, traffic is highest on weekday mornings ‚ÄĒ these are seasonal patterns.

Seasonal decomposition separates a series into three components: Y‚āú = T‚āú + S‚āú + R‚āú (additive) or Y‚āú = T‚āú ¬∑ S‚āú ¬∑ R‚āú (multiplicative). The additive model is appropriate when the seasonal amplitude is constant regardless of the trend level; the multiplicative model is appropriate when the seasonal amplitude grows proportionally with the trend (common for economic and financial series).

Seasonal adjustment removes the seasonal component to reveal the underlying trend and cycle ‚ÄĒ essential for comparing months or quarters that differ in 'expected' seasonal behavior (e.g., unemployment in January vs July). Major statistical agencies (BLS, Eurostat) use X-13ARIMA-SEATS or STL decomposition for official seasonal adjustment of GDP, unemployment, and CPI.

whenWhy.title: Decomposition for understanding and visualization
whenWhy.body: STL (Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess, Cleveland et al. 1990) decomposes Y‚āú = T‚āú + S‚āú + R‚āú using locally weighted regression (loess). Advantages over classical decomposition: handles any seasonal period, robust to outliers, seasonal component can change over time. Classical decomposition uses moving averages for the trend ‚ÄĒ inflexible and cannot handle changing seasonality.

whenWhy.title: Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) modeling
whenWhy.body: SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s explicitly models seasonal autocorrelation. Seasonal differencing ‚ąáňĘY‚āú = Y‚āú ‚ąí Y‚āú‚āč‚āõ removes seasonal non-stationarity. The 'airline model' SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1)‚āĀ‚āā is a parsimonious but effective model for monthly economic data. For weekly data with multiple seasonal periods (e.g., daily, weekly, annual cycles), TBATS or Prophet handles multiple seasonalities.

whenWhy.title: Seasonal dummy variables in regression
whenWhy.body: For regression with seasonal data: include K‚ąí1 seasonal dummy variables (K = number of seasons, drop one to avoid multicollinearity). For monthly data: 11 monthly dummies. Dummy coefficient interpretation: seasonal effect relative to the omitted reference month. For multiplicative seasonality: log-transform Y‚āú and use additive dummies on the log scale. More flexible than fixed seasonal dummies: Fourier series terms sin(2ŌÄkt/s), cos(2ŌÄkt/s) for k = 1,‚Ä¶,K/2.

deepDiveIntro: Additive decomposition: Y‚āú = T‚āú + S‚āú + R‚āú. Seasonal component S‚āú: periodic with period s, ő£‚āĖ‚āĆ‚āÄňĘ‚ĀĽ¬ĻS‚āú‚āč‚āĖ = 0 (zero sum over each season). Estimated via moving average for T‚āú, then average of Y‚āú‚ąíT‚āú within each season for S‚āú, residual R‚āú = Y‚āú ‚ąí T‚āú ‚ąí S‚āú. Multiplicative: Y‚āú = T‚āú ¬∑ S‚āú ¬∑ R‚āú ‚ÄĒ equivalent to additive after log transformation.

STL decomposition (Cleveland et al. 1990): iterates between seasonal smoother (loess within each season across time) and trend smoother (loess of seasonally adjusted series). Two tuning parameters: seasonal window n_s (larger ‚Üí smoother seasonal component), trend window n_t (larger ‚Üí smoother trend). Robust option: down-weight observations with large residuals. STL-ETS or STL-ARIMA: decompose with STL, forecast seasonally adjusted series with ETS or ARIMA, add back seasonal component.

deepDive.heading: Classical Decomposition
deepDive.body: 1. Estimate trend T‚āú using a 2√ós moving average (for even s: 2√ós MA = [0.5Y‚āú‚āč‚āõ/‚āā + Y‚āú‚āč‚āõ/‚āā‚āä‚āĀ+‚Ä¶+Y‚āú‚āä‚āõ/‚āā‚āč‚āĀ + 0.5Y‚āú‚āä‚āõ/‚āā]/s). 2. Detrend: d‚āú = Y‚āú ‚ąí T‚āú (additive) or Y‚āú/T‚āú (multiplicative). 3. Seasonal indices: average d‚āú within each season k = 1,‚Ä¶,s; normalize to sum to 0 (additive) or s (multiplicative). Limitations: trend undefined at series ends (¬Īs/2 observations); assumes constant seasonal pattern; sensitive to outliers. [1]
deepDive.formula: Additive: Y‚āú = T‚āú + S‚āú + R‚āú;   Multiplicative: Y‚āú = T‚āú ¬∑ S‚āú ¬∑ R‚āú

deepDive.heading: STL Decomposition
deepDive.body: STL iterates (outer loop: robustness; inner loop: component estimation): (1) Detrend: d‚āú = Y‚āú ‚ąí T‚āú. (2) Cycle-subseries smoothing: apply loess to each season's subseries {d‚āú: t mod s = k} across time to get raw seasonal. (3) Low-pass filter the raw seasonal and subtract to remove trend leakage. (4) Update seasonal: S‚āú from smoothed minus filtered. (5) Update trend: loess smooth of Y‚āú ‚ąí S‚āú. Repeat. Result: T‚āú, S‚āú, R‚āú with R‚āú = Y‚āú ‚ąí T‚āú ‚ąí S‚āú. Seasonal component can change slowly over years. [1]

deepDive.heading: Seasonal ARIMA: SARIMA
deepDive.body: SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s: ő¶(BňĘ)ŌÜ(B)(1‚ąíBňĘ)Šīį(1‚ąíB)ŠĶąY‚āú = c + őė(BňĘ)őł(B)őĶ‚āú. Seasonal difference ‚ąáňĘ = 1‚ąíBňĘ: ‚ąáňĘY‚āú = Y‚āú ‚ąí Y‚āú‚āč‚āõ removes seasonal non-stationarity. Example: SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)‚āĀ‚āā for monthly series with trend and seasonality. ACF/PACF patterns: seasonal AR shows spikes at lags s, 2s,‚Ä¶ with decay; seasonal MA shows single spike at lag s. Identify seasonal orders by looking at ACF/PACF at multiples of s after seasonal differencing. [1]

deepDive.heading: Fourier Terms for Flexible Seasonality
deepDive.body: For complex or multiple seasonality, include Fourier terms as regressors: sin(2ŌÄkt/s), cos(2ŌÄkt/s) for k = 1,‚Ä¶,K, where s is the seasonal period. K Fourier pairs approximate a smooth seasonal pattern ‚ÄĒ fewer parameters than s‚ąí1 seasonal dummies and can model non-standard periods (e.g., weekly seasonality in daily data: s = 7, or annual in daily data: s = 365.25). TBATS (Trigonometric Box-Cox ARMA Trend Seasonality) and Prophet use this approach for multiple seasonal periods. [1]

takeaways: Additive decomposition: Y‚āú = T‚āú + S‚āú + R‚āú; use when seasonal amplitude is constant. [1]
Multiplicative: Y‚āú = T‚āú¬∑S‚āú¬∑R‚āú (or log-transform ‚Üí additive); use when amplitude grows with trend. [1]
STL: robust loess-based decomposition; handles changing seasonality and any seasonal period. [1]
SARIMA: add seasonal AR(P), MA(Q), and seasonal differencing (D) to ARIMA. [1]
Fourier terms: K pairs of sin/cos allow flexible seasonal patterns with fewer parameters than dummies. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle multiple seasonal periods (e.g., hourly data with daily and weekly seasonality)?
edgeCases.a: Standard ARIMA/SARIMA handles only one seasonal period. For multiple seasonalities: (1) TBATS (De Livera et al. 2011): models multiple seasonal periods with trigonometric Fourier terms, Box-Cox transformation, and ARMA errors ‚ÄĒ handles complex seasonality automatically; (2) Prophet (Facebook/Meta): uses Fourier series for trend, yearly, weekly, and custom seasonalities, with changepoints for trend; (3) STL with multiple seasonal periods (MSTL): iterative decomposition removing each seasonal component in turn. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the X-13ARIMA-SEATS method used by statistical agencies?
edgeCases.a: X-13ARIMA-SEATS is the US Census Bureau's seasonal adjustment software, combining two approaches: (1) X-11: filter-based multiplicative decomposition using Henderson moving averages ‚ÄĒ no parametric model assumptions, robust; (2) SEATS: signal extraction based on ARIMA model ‚ÄĒ projects component models from an estimated ARIMA. X-13 first identifies an ARIMA model (RegARIMA step, allowing for calendar effects, outliers, and regressors), then applies X-11 or SEATS for decomposition. Eurostat uses X-13ARIMA-SEATS for all EU official statistics. [1]

sources.label: Cleveland et al. (1990) ‚ÄĒ STL: A Seasonal-Trend Decomposition Procedure Based on Loess, J. Official Statistics 6(1)
Hyndman & Athanasopoulos ‚ÄĒ Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed.), Ch. 3, 9
